compile.output: Compilation successful with O3 optimization and native ISA (AVX-512 capable)

run.output: Program executed without crashing; returned 10 rows in 3285 ms

validation.comparison_result: Q3: 10 rows returned but all rows have incorrect data. 3 row mismatches confirmed by compare_results.py. Orderkey overlap with ground truth: 0/10. Expected revenue range: 415K-440K; actual revenue range: 489K-524K. Expected dates: 1995-01-27 through 1995-03-14; actual dates: all 1994-01-01 or 1995-01-01.

Q3.notes: Query returns correct row count (10) but with completely wrong data. Root cause: storage encoding mismatch. GenDB storage encodes dates as year-only values (1992-1998) instead of epoch days. Iteration 2 code attempted workaround using lenient year-based filters (o_orderdate < 1996, l_shipdate > 1994) but this includes too many rows and produces wrong aggregations. Expected output has specific dates (1995-02-23, 1995-01-27, etc.) but storage only has year granularity. Fundamental data quality issue: either (a) storage should contain epoch days but was ingested incorrectly, or (b) ground truth expectations are based on different date encoding than what's stored.

Q3.root_cause: Date encoding mismatch combined with insufficient date granularity. Iter 1 identified date encoding issue (code expected epoch days, storage contains years). Iter 2 attempted fix by using year values (1992-1998) with lenient boundaries: o_orderdate < 1996 (includes 1992-1995) and l_shipdate > 1994 (includes 1995-1998). However: (1) This includes ALL rows from these years, not the specific dates required by the SQL (o_orderdate < '1995-03-15', l_shipdate > '1995-03-15'). (2) Storage has only year granularity; GenDB metadata defines dates as int32_t encoding='none' with no comment about year-only format. (3) Code generator prompt specifies dates should be 'days since 1970-01-01' (epoch days). (4) Hexdump confirms storage contains 0x07c8=1992, 0x07cb=1995, etc. (years not epoch days). (5) Code calculates wrong revenue values because it includes too many/wrong lineitem rows. (6) All 10 output orderkeys are different from expected orderkeys; no overlap. Expected revenue: 415K-440K per row; actual: 489K-524K per row. This indicates fundamentally different set of rows being aggregated.

analysis.overall: Critical semantic correctness failure. Iteration 2 attempted to work around storage's year-only date encoding by using lenient year-based filters, but this produces completely wrong results. The query returns 10 rows with correct structure but wrong data: (1) orderkeys don't match expected set (0% overlap), (2) revenue values are ~20% higher than expected, (3) dates are all Jan 1st or placeholder dates instead of specific mid-month dates. The fundamental issue is that GenDB storage contains only year granularity (1992-1998) but the TPC-H query requires day-level filtering ('1995-03-15'). Without day-level date information in storage, it is mathematically impossible to produce the correct results. This is a data ingestion/schema issue, not a query optimization issue.

critical_fixes.issue: Storage date encoding mismatch: GenDB stores dates as year-only values (int32_t with range 1992-1998) instead of epoch days (int32_t with range 8035+). Code generator expects epoch days per prompt documentation. Iteration 2 attempted workaround with year-based filtering but produces wrong results: all 10 orderkeys differ from expected, revenue is ~20% higher, dates are incorrect.
critical_fixes.fix: CRITICAL DECISION REQUIRED: (A) If storage is supposed to contain epoch days: Re-ingest TPC-H data with proper date conversion. Current ingestion stored year values instead of epoch days (e.g., 1995 instead of 9204 for 1995-03-15). This is a data loading bug. (B) If year-only encoding is intentional: Cannot be fixed at code generation level. The SQL query requires day-level granularity ('1995-03-15') which cannot be extracted from year-only values. Would require modifying ground truth expectations or accepting year-level filtering (impossible to match current expected output). Recommend path (A): Fix data ingestion to convert dates to epoch days, then regenerate ground truth to match.
critical_fixes.description: Current storage analysis via hexdump: orders_o_orderdate.col and lineitem_l_shipdate.col contain int32_t values in range [0x07c8, 0x07ce] = [1992, 1998], NOT epoch days. Code generator prompt (src/gendb/agents/code-generator/prompt.md) explicitly states 'Storage format: days since 1970-01-01 (epoch days), Example: 1995-03-15 = 9204 days'. Expected epoch days: 1992-01-01 = 8035, 1995-03-15 = 9204. Iteration 1 discovered this mismatch. Iteration 2 code lines 120-128 attempted workaround: const int32_t orderdate_cutoff = 1996; // < 1996 means years 1992-1995, const int32_t shipdate_cutoff = 1994; // > 1994 means years 1995-1998. But this filters wrong sets of rows, producing 0% orderkey overlap with expected results. Recommendation: (1) Check TPC-H data ingestion process (data loading code) to verify why epoch day conversion was not applied. (2) Re-ingest with proper date conversion: for each date string like '1995-03-15', convert to days-since-1970 = 9204. (3) Regenerate ground truth queries using corrected storage. (4) Re-generate Q3 code which will then use correct epoch day cutoffs.

summary: Q3 iteration 2 fails due to storage data quality issue, not code optimization opportunity. Compilation succeeds (pass), execution runs without crashes (pass), but query correctness is completely broken (fail). Returns 10 rows with zero expected orderkeys and ~20% wrong revenue values. Root cause: GenDB storage encodes dates as year-only values (1992-1998) but code generator and ground truth expect epoch days (8035+). Iteration 2 attempted workaround with lenient year-based filtering but this includes wrong row sets, producing different orderkeys and revenues than expected. This is not a performance bottleneck but a fundamental data encoding mismatch. Cannot fix at code generation level without either: (A) re-ingesting data with proper epoch day conversion, or (B) accepting year-only granularity (which makes expected output unachievable). Recommend immediate investigation of TPC-H data loading pipeline to understand why dates were stored as year values instead of epoch days.
